questions.question: During an MBES patch test, why must latency be solved before the angular offsets (roll, pitch, heading)?

answerOptions.text: Uncorrected latency introduces along-track position errors that contaminate the pitch and heading solutions
answerOptions.rationale: Latency causes position displacement proportional to speed, which directly affects the determination of pitch (along-track on slopes) and heading offsets. Solving it first removes this contamination.

answerOptions.text: Latency is the largest offset and must be removed first for safety
answerOptions.rationale: Latency is not necessarily the largest offset. The reason is that uncorrected latency propagates errors into subsequent angular solutions.

answerOptions.text: The processing software requires latency input before it can calculate angular values
answerOptions.rationale: This is a software limitation argument, not the fundamental reason. The real issue is error propagation in the data signatures.

answerOptions.text: Angular offsets cannot be measured until the vessel speed is known
answerOptions.rationale: Vessel speed is known independently from navigation. The issue is that uncorrected timing errors contaminate the angular offset data signatures.

questions.hint: Think about what happens to sounding positions along-track when there is a timing error, and how that affects slope-based measurements.

questions.question: What does a 'frown' pattern in MBES outer beams on a flat seabed indicate?

answerOptions.text: The applied sound velocity profile is too fast (higher than actual water column conditions)
answerOptions.rationale: When the applied SVP is faster than reality, the system under-corrects for refraction, bending outer beams too far downward, producing a frown. Memory aid: Frown = Fast.

answerOptions.text: The applied sound velocity profile is too slow (lower than actual water column conditions)
answerOptions.rationale: An SVP that is too slow causes a 'smile' pattern where outer beams curve upward. Smile = Slow.

answerOptions.text: There is a residual roll offset in the transducer mounting
answerOptions.rationale: A roll offset produces a symmetric divergence (bowtie/butterfly pattern) between reciprocal lines, not a consistent curvature on all lines.

answerOptions.text: The heave filter is incorrectly configured for the vessel size
answerOptions.rationale: Heave filter issues produce oscillating depth differences correlated with sea state, not a consistent smile or frown curvature pattern.

questions.hint: Remember the memory aid for SVP-induced swath curvature patterns.

questions.question: For side scan sonar operations, what is the recommended towfish altitude as a percentage of the range setting?

answerOptions.rationale: Operating at 10-15% of the range setting balances coverage width, nadir gap size, and image quality. For a 100 m range, this means 10-15 m altitude.
25-30% would be too high, resulting in a very wide nadir gap and weak returns at long range.
5% would be dangerously close to the seabed, risking mechanical damage and providing minimal shadow contrast for target detection.
50% is far too high. At this altitude, the nadir gap would dominate the swath and image quality would be severely degraded.

questions.hint: Think about the balance between coverage width, nadir gap, and image quality.

questions.question: What is the correct formula for converting slant range to ground range in side scan sonar data?

answerOptions.rationale: This Pythagorean relationship removes the vertical component (altitude) from the direct distance (slant range) to give the horizontal distance along the seabed.
Simple subtraction does not account for the geometric relationship. The correction involves trigonometry, not linear subtraction.
Altitude is a distance, not an angle. This formula structure does not apply here.
Adding the squares would give a longer distance, not a shorter one. Ground range must be shorter than slant range.

questions.hint: Consider the right triangle formed by the transducer, the seabed point directly below, and the target on the seabed.

questions.question: During a HydroPACT pipetracker verification, background compensation measurements must not deviate by more than what threshold?

answerOptions.rationale: The background compensation establishes the signal level when no target is nearby. Individual measurements deviating beyond +/-10 uV indicate interference or unsuitable conditions.
+/-20 uV is the threshold for post-compensation coil values after departing from the background compensation location, not for the compensation measurements themselves.
This is tighter than the actual specification. The acceptance criterion for background compensation is +/-10 uV.
This is far too loose. At +/-50 uV deviation, the background compensation would be unreliable.

questions.hint: There are two different uV thresholds in the pipetracker verification -- one for background compensation measurements and one for post-compensation values.

questions.question: Which sound velocity formula is recommended for depths greater than 1000 m?

answerOptions.rationale: Del Grosso (1974) is recommended for depths greater than 1000 m because it performs better at high pressures than Chen & Millero.
Chen & Millero is recommended for depths less than 1000 m. At greater depths, Del Grosso is preferred.
The Wilson equation is not among the recommended formulas in the standard SV operations guidance.
The Mackenzie equation is not referenced in the standard SV operations guidance. Del Grosso is the recommended formula for deep water.

questions.hint: Think about which formula handles high pressure (deep water) conditions better.

questions.question: In a chirp sub-bottom profiler, what primarily determines the vertical resolution of the system?

answerOptions.text: The bandwidth of the frequency sweep (wider bandwidth = better resolution)
answerOptions.rationale: Chirp systems use matched filtering to compress a long swept pulse into a short pulse. The bandwidth determines the compressed pulse length. A 2-8 kHz sweep gives better resolution than a 2-6 kHz sweep.

answerOptions.text: The maximum frequency of the sweep only
answerOptions.rationale: Resolution depends on the bandwidth (difference between start and end frequency), not just the maximum frequency.
Power affects penetration depth and signal-to-noise ratio, not vertical resolution.
Ping rate affects spatial sampling along-track, not the vertical resolution of individual returns.

questions.hint: Consider what happens during the matched filtering process and how the sweep range relates to the compressed pulse.

questions.question: What is the minimum required signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for a valid surrogate item detection using the GMA1000 gradiometer?

answerOptions.text: Greater than 3:1 (target amplitude vs noise floor)
answerOptions.rationale: A valid detection requires the target dipolar anomaly amplitude to be at least 3 times the ambient noise floor. Below this, detection is considered ambiguous.
2:1 is insufficient. The acceptance criterion requires a minimum SNR of 3:1 for reliable target detection.
While 5:1 would be excellent, the minimum acceptance criterion is 3:1.
10:1 is well above the minimum requirement. The pass criterion is SNR > 3:1.

questions.hint: The acceptance criteria specify a minimum ratio between target amplitude and the noise floor baseline.

questions.question: When measuring draft for MBES operations, what two vessel dynamics must be considered in addition to the static draft measurement?

answerOptions.text: Squat (trim change between at-rest and underway) and settlement (elevation change between at-rest and underway)
answerOptions.rationale: Squat is the change in vessel trim (stern lowers, bow rises) when underway. Settlement is the overall lowering of the hull when moving. Both change the effective transducer depth.
Heave and roll are vessel motions compensated by the MRU in real-time. Draft dynamics are squat and settlement.
Pitch and yaw are compensated by the motion sensor and gyro. The draft-related dynamics are squat and settlement.
Tidal reduction is a separate correction applied to depth measurements. Draft dynamics are squat and settlement.

questions.hint: These are changes in the vessel's waterplane relationship that occur when it transitions from stationary to underway.

questions.question: For the Voyis Observer Pro 3 camera array, what is the maximum acceptable clock drift between cameras and the INS over the duration of a dive?

answerOptions.text: Less than 500 ms total drift over the dive duration
answerOptions.rationale: Camera clocks can drift several hundred milliseconds over long dives (8+ hours). Less than 500 ms total drift is the acceptance criterion. Pre-dive sync must be within 20 ms.

answerOptions.text: Less than 20 ms total drift over the dive duration
answerOptions.rationale: 20 ms is the pre-dive synchronisation requirement, not the post-dive drift tolerance.

answerOptions.text: Less than 1 second total drift over the dive duration
answerOptions.rationale: 1 second of drift could produce significant position errors at survey speed. The acceptance criterion is 500 ms.

answerOptions.text: Less than 100 ms total drift over the dive duration
answerOptions.rationale: While 100 ms would be excellent, the acceptance criterion allows up to 500 ms of drift over a dive.

questions.hint: Think about how drift accumulates over an 8+ hour dive and what position error it produces at typical survey speed.

questions.question: During an SVP repeatability test, what is the maximum acceptable disagreement between two consecutive casts at equivalent depths?

answerOptions.rationale: Two consecutive SVP casts at the same location should agree within 0.5 m/s. Values between 0.5 and 1.0 m/s are marginal and require investigation. Greater than 1.0 m/s is a fail.
0.1 m/s is tighter than the acceptance criterion. The pass threshold is 0.5 m/s.
1.0 m/s is the fail threshold. Values between 0.5 and 1.0 m/s are marginal.
2.0 m/s far exceeds the acceptable range. The pass criterion is 0.5 m/s.

questions.hint: The test has three categories: accept, marginal, and fail.

questions.question: What is the TVU formula used in IHO S-44 Edition 6.1.0?

answerOptions.rationale: This formula combines a depth-independent coefficient (a) and a depth-dependent coefficient (b x d) in a root-sum-square, giving the maximum allowable TVU at the 95% confidence level.
The TVU formula is not a simple linear sum. It uses a root-sum-square combination of the two terms.
This gives the squared value. The actual TVU is the square root of this expression.
This is not the correct IHO S-44 formula. The correct formula is the root-sum-square of a and (b x d).

questions.hint: It combines a constant term and a depth-proportional term using root-sum-square.

questions.question: In a magnetometer acceptance test, what is the purpose of running reciprocal (opposite direction) lines over the same target?

answerOptions.text: To expose any heading-dependent bias in the data and verify position consistency
answerOptions.rationale: Magnetometer sensors are sensitive to tow platform heading. Reciprocal lines reveal heading-dependent offsets and confirm target position consistency regardless of approach direction.

answerOptions.text: To double the amount of data collected for statistical significance
answerOptions.rationale: The primary purpose is to detect heading-dependent systematic errors, not simply increase sample size.

answerOptions.text: To cancel out the effects of ocean current on the tow cable
answerOptions.rationale: Current effects are a practical concern, but the main purpose is detecting heading-dependent bias.

answerOptions.text: To measure the Earth's diurnal magnetic variation
answerOptions.rationale: Diurnal variation is monitored separately. Reciprocal lines detect heading-dependent bias.

questions.hint: Think about what systematic errors become visible only when comparing data from opposite approach directions.

questions.question: When deploying an SVP probe, how long should the instrument be held near the surface before beginning the cast?

answerOptions.text: 10 minutes to acquire ambient water temperature
answerOptions.rationale: The probe needs time to thermally equilibrate with the water, which may differ significantly from air temperature on deck. A 10-minute surface soak prevents false SV readings.

answerOptions.text: 2 minutes to verify the probe is working
answerOptions.rationale: 2 minutes is insufficient for thermal equilibration. The standard procedure requires 10 minutes.

answerOptions.text: 30 seconds to clear any air bubbles
answerOptions.rationale: While air bubbles are a concern, the 10-minute soak is primarily for thermal equilibration.

answerOptions.text: No wait time is needed; begin the cast immediately
answerOptions.rationale: Casting immediately produces false readings because the sensor starts at air temperature rather than water temperature.

questions.hint: The sensor needs to transition from air temperature to water temperature.

questions.question: For the SSS positioning verification test, what is identified as the single most frequent cause of position errors?

answerOptions.rationale: A wrong heading source rotates all contacts around the vehicle position, producing large cross-track errors that increase with range.
Offset errors cause position displacement, but heading source error is identified as the most frequent cause.
Incorrect SV affects slant-to-ground range conversion, but heading source error is the most common cause.
Time sync is important, but heading source error is explicitly identified as the single most frequent cause.

questions.hint: Think about which error rotates all contacts around the vehicle position.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of parametric sub-bottom profilers over conventional chirp systems?

answerOptions.text: They produce a very narrow beam width (3-5 deg) from a low-frequency pulse, giving high resolution with a small footprint
answerOptions.rationale: Parametric systems use non-linear interaction of two high-frequency beams to generate a low-frequency difference pulse with the narrow beam width of the primaries (3-5 deg vs 15-40 deg for conventional), making them ideal for ROV operations.

answerOptions.text: They achieve much greater penetration depth in all sediment types
answerOptions.rationale: Penetration is similar. The advantage is the narrow beam width achieved through non-linear acoustic interaction.

answerOptions.text: They require less power than chirp systems
answerOptions.rationale: Power requirements are not the distinguishing feature. The key advantage is the narrow beam width.

answerOptions.text: They can operate at higher survey speeds
answerOptions.rationale: Survey speed is not the primary advantage. The narrow beam width and small footprint are the key benefits.

questions.hint: Consider how the beam width is achieved and why that matters for ROV-mounted operations.

questions.question: In the SV/CTD comparison verification, what is the maximum acceptable difference between CTD-derived sound velocity and directly measured SVP sound velocity?

answerOptions.text: Less than or equal to 1.0 m/s at all depths
answerOptions.rationale: The CTD-derived SV vs direct SVP tolerance is 1.0 m/s, which accounts for the uncertainty introduced by the empirical equation used to calculate SV from conductivity, temperature, and pressure.

answerOptions.text: Less than or equal to 0.5 m/s at all depths
answerOptions.rationale: 0.5 m/s is the tolerance for comparing two direct SVP units against each other, not for CTD-derived vs direct SVP.

answerOptions.text: Less than or equal to 0.1 m/s at all depths
answerOptions.rationale: 0.1 m/s is tighter than the acceptance criterion. The CTD-derived vs direct SVP tolerance accounts for formula uncertainty.

answerOptions.text: Less than or equal to 2.0 m/s at all depths
answerOptions.rationale: 2.0 m/s is too loose. The acceptance criterion is 1.0 m/s for CTD-derived vs direct SVP comparison.

questions.hint: The tolerance for derived vs direct measurement is wider than for two direct measurements, because of the equation uncertainty.

questions.question: A project requires depth measurement in 500 m of water. You select a Paroscientific Digiquartz sensor with a 0-700 m FSD range. What is the absolute accuracy of this sensor?

answerOptions.rationale: Absolute accuracy is quoted as a percentage of the Full Scale Deflection. For the Paroscientific 8000 series, this is 0.01% of FSD. 0.01% x 700 m = 0.07 m.
Absolute accuracy is based on the FSD (700 m), not the actual working depth (500 m). The sensor is calibrated over its full range.
The Paroscientific 8000 series has 0.01% accuracy, not 0.1%. That would be an order of magnitude worse.
0.001% is the resolution specification, not the absolute accuracy. Absolute accuracy is 0.01% of FSD.

questions.hint: Absolute accuracy is a percentage of FSD, not working depth. What percentage does the Paroscientific 8000 series specify?

questions.question: When configuring a depth sensor for ROV deployment, what atmospheric pressure range in PSI should you expect to see on deck as a healthy sanity check?

answerOptions.rationale: Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is approximately 14.7 PSI (1013.25 mbar). The range of 13.78 to 15.23 PSI covers normal atmospheric variation. If the sensor reads in this range on deck, it is functioning correctly.
0-1 PSI would indicate the sensor is reading near zero, suggesting it is already atmospherically compensated or malfunctioning. On deck, it should read atmospheric pressure.
100-150 PSI would correspond to approximately 70-100 m of water depth. This would indicate a faulty sensor or incorrect configuration.
29-31 is the atmospheric pressure range in inches of mercury (inHg), not PSI. In PSI the range is approximately 13.78 to 15.23.

questions.hint: Standard atmospheric pressure is about 1013.25 mbar. What is that in PSI?

questions.question: In a closed loop depth measurement, the misclosure between start and end readings at the benchmark is +0.30 m over 15 minutes. What correction should be applied to a reading taken 5 minutes into the loop?

answerOptions.rationale: The correction formula is: -1 x (time difference / total time) x total misclosure. So: -1 x (5/15) x 0.30 = -0.10 m. The misclosure is distributed linearly over time.
This would apply the full misclosure to every reading, rather than distributing it proportionally over time.
This would be -1 x (5/15) x 0.45, not the correct calculation. The time ratio of 5/15 applied to 0.30 m gives 0.10 m.
This uses the wrong time ratio. The correct calculation is -1 x (5/15) x 0.30 = -0.10 m.

questions.hint: The correction formula distributes the misclosure linearly over time: -1 x (time difference / total time) x total misclosure.

questions.question: Why must depth sensor closed loops NOT be conducted within 30 minutes of high or low tide?

answerOptions.text: The tide changes direction near high/low water, making linear interpolation of the tidal correction unreliable
answerOptions.rationale: Closed loops assume a linear tidal change over the loop duration. Near the tidal stand (high or low water), the rate of change is non-linear, which invalidates the linear correction model.

answerOptions.text: The water is too rough near high and low tide to take accurate readings
answerOptions.rationale: Sea state is not directly related to tidal state. The issue is the non-linear tidal rate near the stand.

answerOptions.text: Atmospheric pressure fluctuates more near tidal extremes
answerOptions.rationale: Atmospheric pressure is independent of tidal state. The issue is the non-linear tidal rate.

answerOptions.text: The density of the water column changes dramatically at tidal extremes
answerOptions.rationale: While density can vary with tidal mixing, the reason for avoiding tidal extremes is the non-linear rate of tidal change.

questions.hint: Think about what assumption the closed loop correction makes about how tide changes over time.

questions.question: Which of the three 4DNAV NavView pressure-to-depth conversion methods provides the most accurate result?

answerOptions.text: Dynamic -- integrates density from the water column profile to the input pressure
answerOptions.rationale: The Dynamic method uses density integrated from the full water column profile, implementing IOGP Report 649 dynamic depth equations. This accounts for actual density variations through the water column rather than using a single mean value.

answerOptions.text: Simple UNESCO -- uses standard world ocean density with pressure and latitude
answerOptions.rationale: Simple UNESCO uses a standard world ocean density (S=35, T=0 C) and does not account for local water column conditions.

answerOptions.text: Density -- uses mean density from CTD profile
answerOptions.rationale: The Density method uses a single mean density value for the entire water column, which is less accurate than integrating the full density profile.

answerOptions.text: All three methods give identical results when properly configured
answerOptions.rationale: The methods differ significantly. Dynamic is the most accurate as it uses integrated density rather than a single value or standard ocean assumption.

questions.hint: Consider which method uses the most complete representation of the water column properties.

questions.question: What is the correlation threshold that must exist between repeated closed loop depth measurements before the loops are considered acceptable?

answerOptions.rationale: Depth loops must be conducted a minimum of twice, or until a correlation of less than 1.5 cm exists between loops. This ensures measurement repeatability for metrology applications.
5 cm is too loose for metrology depth loops. The acceptance criterion is 1.5 cm.
0.5 cm would be extremely tight and difficult to achieve in practice. The criterion is 1.5 cm.
10 cm far exceeds the acceptance criterion. The threshold is 1.5 cm between loops.

questions.hint: This threshold relates to metrology depth loop repeatability.
